rulings.Case Title: KEVIN WILLIAMS VS. ERIC SAFIRE et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Or Alternatively Tax Costs
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 26, 2015, Line 1, PLAINTIFF KEVIN WILLIAMS' Motion To Strike Or Alternatively Tax Costs. Plaintiff's motion to strike costs is granted in part. CCP § 1032 states that costs are available as a matter of right when the prevailing party is within one of the four categories designated by statute. (§ 1032, subds. (a)(4), (b).) § 1032 includes four categories of parties: (1) the party with a net monetary recovery, (2) the defendant in whose favor a dismissal was entered, (3) the defendant where neither plaintiff nor defendant recovers any relief, and (4) the defendant against whom plaintiff has not recovered any relief. (See Michell v. Olick (1996) 49 Cal. App. 4th 1194, 1197-98.) Here, dismissal was entered in favor of Defendantson March 20, 2015, therefore Defendants are within CCP § 1032. § 1033.5, subdivision (a), lists specific items of costs which may be recovered as a matter of right pursuant to section §1032. Among allowable costs are filing fees, jury fees and juror food, deposition expenses, service of process costs, witness fees, transcripts order ed by the court, and expenses in preparing blowups and photocopies of exhibits. Here, Defendants' request filing fees and deposition costs. These fall under § 1033.5, subdivision (a), therefore Defendants are entitled to recover these costs and Plaintiff's motion as to these costs is denied. The Court finds Defendants are not entitled to recover costs associated with arbitration. As to the matter of costs associated with arbitration, the court orders the parties to attend the hearing and be prepared to discuss what costs have been incurred since the May 2008 Arbitration award. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/SB)

rulings.Case Title: GINO BRIZZOLARA VS. BAY CAB et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Wednesday, August 26, 2015, Line 2, DEFENDANT KHANH VU HUYNH'S MOTION TO SET ASIDE DEFAULT/DEFAULT JUDGMENT/LEAVE TO DEFEND. Defendant's Notion of Motion and Motion for Order Vacating and Setting Aside Default and Default Judgment is DENIED. Defendant Huynh fails to meet his burden of proof to set aside the entry of default judgment established in In re Marriage of Kieturakis (2006) 138 Cal.App.4th 56, 80. Defendant Huynh's reply does not contest Plaintiff's allegation Defendant Huynh resided at 1435 5th Avenue in Oakland four days before substituted service occurred at that resident. In addition, Defendant Huynh provides no copies of lease agreements, mail received at another address, or declarations by third parties to demonstrate that he did not live at 1435 5th Avenue in Oakland in 2011. In the absence of evidence of extrinsic fraud or mistake, applications for relief from default based upon excusable neglect must be brought within six-months after an entry of default. (Marianos v. Tutunjian (1977) 70 Cal. App. 3d 61, 64; Cal. Rules of Court, rules 62, 63.) Here, Defendant Huynh seeks to set aside a default judgment more than a year after this court entered default judgment. Defendant Huynh provides no justification for missing the six month deadline. "To warrant relief under section 473 a litigant's neglect must have been such as might have been the act of a reasonably prudent person under the same circumstances. The inadvertence contemplated by the statute does not mean mere inadvertence in the abstract. If it is wholly inexcusable it does not justify relief." (Hearn v. Howards (2009) 177 Cal.App.4th 1193, 1206.) Defendant Huyhn provides no evidence that Plaintiff's counsel told him "not to worry" about the lawsuit. Even if they had, no reasonable person would have declined to participate in litigation in which they are a named defendant because plaintiff's counsel said "not to worry." rulings.Case Title: JENI YANG VS. LEVI STRAUSS & CO. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Compliance With Deposition Notice Requiring (I) Identification Of Potential Witnesses And (2) Production Of Documents (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, August 26, 2015, Line 3, Motion To Compel Compliance With Deposition Notice Requiring (I) Identification Of Potential Witnesses And (2) Production Of Documents (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only). (Continued from Part 1 of 2) 3. Defendant shall notify former employees previously identified that plaintiff seeks their home and work addresses and telephone numbers and shall afford such former employees with an opportunity to object in writing to disclosure thereof; 4. Defendant shall notify job applicants previously identified that plaintiff seeks their home and work addresses and telephone numbers and shall afford such job applicants with an opportunity to object in writing to disclosure thereof; 5. Defendant shall produce witness identification information in the categories set forth above except to the extent that the witnesses in question have objected in writing to such disclosure. 6. Defendant produce prior complaints of pregnancy discrimination or sex discrimination based on pregnancy for the last ten years provided that defendant shall notify prior complainants that plaintiff seeks disclosure of documents which may contain identifying information and shall afford such prior complainants with an opportunity to object in writing to disclosure thereof; 7. Plaintiff's request for an award of sanctions is denied. rulings.Case Title: JENI YANG VS. LEVI STRAUSS & CO. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Compel Compliance With Deposition Notice Requiring (I) Identification Of Potential Witnesses And (2) Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, August 26, 2015, Line 3, PLAINTIFF JENI YANG'S Motion To Compel Compliance With Deposition Notice Requiring (I) Identification Of Potential Witnesses And (2) Production Of Documents (Part 1 of 2 For Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling Only). Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Information pertaining to non-parties may be within the recognized "zone of privacy" and subject to protection from discovery. Coito v. Superior Court (State of Calif.), 54 Cal. 4th 480, 502 (2012). Such information may be discovered provided the non-parties are given the opportunity to object to disclosure. Pioneer Electronics (USA) Inc. v. Superior Court (Olmstead), 40 Cal.4th 360, 367 (2007). However, the Court does not find such a privacy interest inherent in the work addresses and telephone numbers of current employees of defendant. With reference to documents pertaining to complaints previously submitted to defendant, the same are discoverable as to claims comparable to those asserted by plaintiff and with the same opportunity for complainants to object to disclosure of contact information. See Pioneer Electronics, supra. The Court does not find the relevance of such pattern and practice information to be bounded by the period of plaintiff's employment because a continuing pattern and practice could have preexisted plaintiff's employment. The ten years of data sought by plaintiff and recognized in the Judicial Council Form Interrogatories is not inherently unreasonable and has not been shown to be so in this case. However, the Court finds plaintiff's prior request for production of prior complaints to be overbroad to the extent it is not limited to complaints of pregnancy discrimination or sex discrimination based on pregnancy as in the case of plaintiff's complaint herein. Based on the foregoing, the Court will grant plaintiff's motion to compel identification of witnesses and production of documents as follows: 1. Defendant shall disclose the work addresses and telephone numbers of current employees previously identified; 2. Defendant shall notify current employees previously identified that plaintiff seeks their home addresses and telephone numbers and shall afford such employees with an opportunity to object in writing to disclosure thereof; (Tentative Ruling Continued in Part 2 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: HRD COFFEE SHOP INC. VS. RICHARD TOM
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Deposition Of Richard Tom And For Protective Order
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, August 26, 2015, Line 4, PLAINTIFFS HRD COFFEE SHOP INC. AND DAVID YEUNG'S Motion To Compel Deposition Of Richard Tom And For Protective Order. Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The Discovery Act does not recognize priority based on notice alone; the fact that a party is conducting discovery, whether by deposition or otherwise, does not operate to delay the discovery of any other party. Defendant Tom's unexplained refusal to appear for a properly noticed deposition until plaintiff Yeung's deposition had been taken nearly a month later was in direct contravention of this established principle. Such an unexplained failure to appear is ordinarily grounds for sanctions. However, sanctions were not requested by plaintiff and therefore cannot be awarded. CCP Section 2023.030. Accordingly, plaintiff's motion to compel is granted. The deposition of defendant Tom shall appear for deposition on the next available date. Plaintiff's motion for protective order is also granted. Plaintiff Yeung shall appear for deposition on the first available date after the deposition of defendant Tom is completed. Defendant Tom's request for sanctions is denied. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jamesfleming@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)
